rows.v: How high or low a sound is, determined by the frequency of its vibration. The foundation of melody and harmony.
She tuned each string until its pitch matched the note exactly.
From Old English roots tied to 'pitching' or throwing — the sense of where a tone is 'placed' on the high-low spectrum. Higher frequency means higher pitch.
The interval between one note and another with double its frequency — they sound like 'the same note' higher or lower. The basic repeating unit of pitch.
He sang the melody an octave higher, the same notes lifted up to a brighter range.
From Latin 'octavus' (eighth) — it spans eight notes of the scale (C to C). The doubling of frequency makes the two notes blend almost into one.
An ordered sequence of notes ascending or descending within an octave, following a specific pattern of steps. The raw material of melody.
The student practiced the C major scale up and down until her fingers knew it cold.
From Latin 'scala' (ladder, staircase) — the notes climb like rungs of a ladder. Different step patterns create major, minor, and other scales.
The group of notes (a scale) a piece is centered on, named for its home note — like 'the key of C.' It defines which notes sound 'at rest.'
The song was in the key of G, so every melody gravitated back home to G.
From the sense of a 'key' that unlocks or organizes — the key establishes the tonal home base around which the whole piece is built.
The distance in pitch between two notes. Intervals (a third, a fifth, an octave) are the building blocks of melody and harmony.
The leap of a perfect fifth gave the opening its bold, heroic sound.
From Latin 'intervallum' (space between) — literally the gap between two pitches. Each interval has its own characteristic sound and emotional color.
A 'sharp' (♯) raises a note by a half step; a 'flat' (♭) lowers it by a half step. They're how music reaches the notes between the natural ones.
He played F-sharp instead of F, nudging the note up a half step to fit the key.
'Sharp' from the sense of a keener, higher edge; 'flat' from the sense of something lowered or dulled — the directions a note's pitch is bent.
A sharp, flat, or natural sign that temporarily changes a note's pitch outside the key signature. Notes that color outside the lines.
The surprising accidental added a touch of tension before resolving back to the key.
From the sense of something incidental or out of the ordinary — these notes fall 'accidentally' outside the piece's established key.
A symbol at the start of the staff that fixes which pitches the lines and spaces represent. The treble and bass clefs are the most common.
She read the high notes in treble clef and the low ones in bass clef.
From French 'clef' (key) — the clef is literally the 'key' that unlocks how to read the notes on the staff. The treble clef descends from a stylized letter G.
The speed of the music — how fast or slow the beats go, often marked in beats per minute or with Italian terms like 'allegro' (fast).
The conductor pushed the tempo, driving the orchestra to a thrilling, breakneck pace.
Italian for 'time'. Tempo markings (largo, andante, allegro, presto) have been written in Italian since the Baroque era, a universal musical language.
The two stacked numbers at the start of a piece showing how beats are grouped — the top counts beats per measure, the bottom names the beat's note value.
In 3/4 time, each measure held three beats, giving the waltz its lilting sway.
Descriptive: it 'signs' the metrical structure of time. 4/4 is so universal it's simply called 'common time', often marked with a 'C'.
The steady, recurring pulse of music — what you tap your foot or clap along to. The fundamental unit of musical time.
She felt the beat in her chest and clapped along on every downbeat.
From the sense of a steady striking or pulsing. The beat is music's heartbeat — the regular pulse everything else is measured against.
A segment of music containing a set number of beats, marked off by vertical bar lines. Also called a 'bar.' How music is divided for reading.
He counted four measures of rest before his entrance.
'Measure' from the sense of a measured portion; 'bar' from the vertical bar lines that fence each one off. The two terms are used interchangeably.
Placing emphasis on the off-beats or weak parts of the rhythm, against the expected pulse. The source of groove in jazz, funk, and much pop.
The syncopation pushed the accent onto the upbeats, giving the tune its irresistible swing.
From Greek 'synkopē' (a cutting short) — the expected strong beat is 'cut' and the stress shifts off-balance, creating rhythmic surprise and drive.
A symbol indicating silence — a measured pause where no note is played. Silence is as much a part of music as sound.
The dramatic rest before the final chord let the tension hang in the air.
From the everyday 'rest' (a pause). Each note value has a matching rest of equal duration; composers shape music with silence as deliberately as with sound.
The recurring pattern of strong and weak beats that organizes rhythm — like the ONE-two-three of a waltz or the ONE-two-three-four of a march.
The piece's triple meter gave it a graceful, circling momentum.
From Greek 'metron' (measure) — the same root as poetic meter. It's the underlying pattern of stressed and unstressed beats that gives music its feel.
Three or more notes played together as a unit. Chords provide the harmonic foundation that supports melody.
He strummed a single rich chord that filled the room with warmth.
From 'accord' (agreement, from Latin 'cor', heart) — the notes are in 'accord', sounding together. The 'a' was dropped over time to give 'chord'.
The most basic chord, built of three notes stacked in thirds — a root, a third, and a fifth. The foundation of Western harmony.
The simple major triad — root, third, and fifth — gave the hymn its bright, stable sound.
From Greek 'trias' (a group of three) — three notes forming the simplest complete chord. Major and minor triads are harmony's basic building blocks.
Two contrasting tonalities: 'major' tends to sound bright, happy, or resolved; 'minor' tends to sound darker, sadder, or tense. The basic emotional colors of music.
Switching from major to minor turned the cheerful tune suddenly melancholy.
'Major' from Latin for 'greater', 'minor' for 'lesser' — referring to the size of the defining third interval. The slight difference carries enormous emotional weight.
A sequence of chords played in succession. Chord progressions create the harmonic journey and emotional arc of a song.
The whole song rode on a four-chord progression that felt instantly familiar.
From 'progress' — the chords 'progress' from one to the next. Certain progressions are so satisfying they recur across thousands of songs.
A chord with a fourth note added a seventh above the root, giving a richer, more colorful, often jazzy or unresolved sound.
The dominant seventh chord ached to resolve, pulling the ear toward home.
Named for the interval of a seventh added atop the basic triad. The added note creates tension and color, central to jazz and blues harmony.
'Dissonance' is a clashing, unstable sound that wants to resolve; 'consonance' is a stable, restful, pleasant sound. The tension and release that drives music.
The dissonance built unbearable tension until it finally melted into a consonant chord.
'Dissonance' from Latin 'dissonare' (to sound apart); 'consonance' from 'consonare' (to sound together). Music breathes through their interplay of tension and rest.
The notes of a chord played one after another instead of all at once — a 'broken chord.' A staple of melody, accompaniment, and technique.
Instead of strumming, she played the chord as a flowing arpeggio, one note rippling into the next.
From Italian 'arpeggiare' (to play the harp) — harpists naturally roll chord notes in sequence, the gesture that gave the technique its name.
A sequence of chords that brings a musical phrase to a close, like punctuation. Cadences signal pauses, questions, or full stops in the music.
The piece ended on a strong, final cadence that left no doubt it was over.
From Latin 'cadere' (to fall) — melodies often 'fall' to rest at a cadence. It's the musical equivalent of a comma, semicolon, or period.
A sequence of single notes heard as a coherent, memorable line — the 'tune' you hum. The part of music that usually sticks in your head.
Everyone left the concert humming the unforgettable melody.
From Greek 'melōidia' (singing, song), from 'melos' (tune) + 'aoidē' (song) — the singable, linear thread that rides atop the harmony.
Notes sounding together to support and enrich the melody. Harmony is the vertical dimension of music — what happens when pitches stack.
The backing singers added lush harmony beneath the lead vocal.
From Greek 'harmonia' (joining, agreement) — the art of combining simultaneous pitches into a pleasing whole, the counterpart to horizontal melody.
The volume of music and how it changes — from soft ('piano') to loud ('forte'), with swells and fades between. Dynamics shape drama and emotion.
The dramatic dynamics swept from a barely-audible whisper to a thunderous climax.
From Greek 'dynamis' (power, force). Marked with Italian terms (pp, p, mf, f, ff), dynamics give music its ebb and flow of intensity.
How individual notes are played and connected — smoothly ('legato'), short and detached ('staccato'), accented, and so on. The phrasing and 'touch' of music.
The crisp staccato articulation gave the passage a light, bouncing character.
From Latin 'articulare' (to divide into joints) — articulation 'joints' the notes, defining how each is attacked, sustained, and released.
A short, recurring musical idea — a few notes or a rhythm — that recurs and develops throughout a piece. A musical building block and signature.
The famous four-note motif returned again and again, transformed each time.
From French 'motif' (motive, theme), from Latin 'motivus' (moving) — a small idea that 'moves' through a work, unifying it. Beethoven's Fifth is the classic example.
Changing from one key to another within a piece. A modulation can lift, darken, or refresh the music as it shifts tonal home.
The final chorus modulated up a step, lifting the song to a triumphant new height.
From Latin 'modulari' (to measure, regulate) — the music is 're-keyed' to a new tonal center. A well-placed modulation can feel like a sudden surge of energy.
The art of combining two or more independent melodies that sound good together. Each line stands alone, yet they interweave into harmony.
Bach's counterpoint wove several independent melodies into a single dazzling whole.
From Latin 'punctus contra punctum' (point against point, i.e. note against note) — the interplay of independent melodic lines, the height of compositional craft.
To 'woodshed' is to practice intensely and privately, drilling difficult material until you master it. The unglamorous grind behind every great performance.
He woodshedded that solo for weeks before he dared play it on stage.
American jazz slang, evoking a musician retreating to a backyard woodshed to practice alone, out of earshot, until the tough passages were conquered.
A musician's technical skill and ability on their instrument — especially speed, precision, and endurance. 'Great chops' means seriously skilled.
The young guitarist had serious chops, ripping through the fast runs effortlessly.
Originally referring to a brass player's lip muscles (their 'chops', as in jaws), it broadened to mean technical command on any instrument.
Slang for a musician's instrument — originally a guitar, but used for saxophones, basses, and others. Your axe is your weapon of choice.
He showed up to the jam session with his axe slung over his shoulder.
Jazz slang dating to the mid-1900s, possibly likening the sweeping motion of playing a sax or the shape of a guitar to wielding an axe.
To play 'in the pocket' is to lock perfectly into the groove and timing, especially in a rhythm section. The feel of everything sitting just right.
The drummer and bassist were so in the pocket that the whole band floated on the groove.
From the sense of fitting snugly 'in the pocket' — the rhythm sits in exactly the right place, tight and effortless, the holy grail of groove.
Playing aimlessly or improvising casually without a real structure — doodling on an instrument. Fun and exploratory, but not focused practice.
He was just noodling on the piano, wandering through ideas without playing any real song.
From 'noodle' in the sense of fiddling or improvising idly — the musical equivalent of doodling, meandering rather than purposeful playing.
A live musical engagement or performance job. From a single bar set to a stadium tour, a gig is any paid playing date.
The band landed a weekend gig at the downtown club.
1920s jazz slang of uncertain origin; it spread from music to mean any job or engagement, eventually lending its name to the whole 'gig economy'.
